proposals.title: A Multi-Compartmental Systems Biology Model to Simulate Coagulation Factor Dynamics and Predict Hemostatic Rebalance in Liver Cirrhosis
proposals.motivation: Patients with liver cirrhosis exhibit a complex 'rebalanced' hemostasis, where both pro- and anti-hemostatic factors are reduced. This fragile balance is clinically significant, as it underlies both bleeding and thrombotic risks, complicating management. Pre-2026 research, including our work on albumin and outcomes in acutely ill patients, highlights the critical role of systemic inflammation and organ dysfunction in shaping patient trajectories, necessitating models that integrate physiology beyond the liver.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a multi-compartmental computational model, incorporating extra-hepatic synthesis and clearance of key factors (e.g., endothelial Protein C, splenic Factor VIII), will accurately simulate the dynamic, rebalanced hemostatic state in cirrhosis and predict how perturbations in one physiological compartment propagate to alter the net coagulation phenotype.
proposals.method: We will develop an organ-level, ordinary differential equation (ODE)-based model using a systems biology approach (e.g., employing COPASI or custom Python/SciPy solvers). The model will explicitly include hepatic, endothelial, and splenic compartments, with kinetic parameters for factor production, consumption, and clearance derived from pre-2026 literature. Sensitivity analysis and parameter fitting will be performed against clinical coagulation factor level datasets from cirrhotic patients to validate the model's predictive capability for static and dynamic scenarios.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first dynamic, multi-compartmental framework to explain hemostatic rebalance in cirrhosis, moving beyond static factor measurements. It will offer a computational tool to test hypotheses about the net effect of modulating extra-hepatic pathways, potentially identifying novel therapeutic targets to stabilize coagulation in this vulnerable population.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the explicit integration of validated extra-hepatic physiological sources and sinks of coagulation factors into a dynamic flux model of the entire cascade, a synthesis not previously attempted to explain the cirrhosis phenotype.

proposals.title: Endothelial-Thrombotic Coupling in Endotoxemia: A Spatially-Resolved Map of Regional Vulnerability Across Vascular Beds
proposals.motivation: Systemic inflammation, as seen in sepsis, COVID-19, and COPD exacerbations, triggers a complex coagulopathy with high mortality. Pre-2026 clinical data, including our own work linking low albumin to thrombotic risk, suggests this is not a uniform process but varies by vascular bed, contributing to organ-specific failure. However, the integrated local circuits of endothelial phenotype, hemodynamics, and soluble mediators that determine regional thrombotic resilience remain undefined.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that endotoxemia induces distinct, bed-specific endothelial transcriptional and functional programs, and that the combination of these programs with local hemodynamic forces predicts regional fibrin deposition, revealing unique therapeutic targets for different vascular territories.
proposals.method: We will use intravital microscopy in C57BL/6 mice with LPS-induced endotoxemia, employing bed-specific fluorescent coagulation sensors (e.g., fibrin-binding antibody) to quantify real-time thrombotic activity in portal, hepatic sinusoidal, and mesenteric vessels. Endothelial cells will be isolated via fluorescence-activated cell sorting from these beds for single-cell RNA sequencing (10x Genomics platform). Hemodynamic parameters (shear stress, flow patterns) will be derived from computational fluid dynamics simulations based on vessel architecture. Data integration will use multivariate regression and network analysis (e.g., in R/Python) to correlate transcriptional modules, shear stress, and plasma markers (including albumin) with fibrin deposition maps.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first high-resolution, mechanistic map linking local endothelial biology to thrombotic vulnerability in systemic inflammation. It will move the field from a paradigm of global coagulopathy to one of regional dysregulation, identifying novel, bed-specific endothelial targets for anticoagulant or endothelial-stabilizing therapies. The findings will directly inform the management of thrombotic risk in acutely ill patients with conditions like sepsis and severe COVID-19.
proposals.novelty: While endothelial dysfunction in sepsis is known, a simultaneous, spatially-resolved analysis of transcriptional programs, hemodynamics, and real-time coagulation across multiple vascular beds has not been performed. This integrative approach moves beyond testing uniform biomarkers to define the combinatorial logic of local thrombotic circuits.

proposals.title: Differential Pro-thrombotic Risk in Portal, Hepatic, and Systemic Circulation: A Retrospective Cohort Study in Acutely Ill Medical Patients
proposals.motivation: Hypercoagulability is a recognized complication in acutely ill patients, including those with COPD and post-COVID-19 syndrome, and is associated with poor outcomes. However, the assumption of a uniform pro-thrombotic state across all vascular beds is clinically unproven. The portal and hepatic circulations have unique hemodynamic and endothelial properties, suggesting thrombotic risk may differ significantly from the systemic circulation, impacting prophylaxis and management strategies.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the pro/anti-coagulant imbalance, as reflected by validated biomarkers and clinical outcomes, manifests with different intensities and consequences in the portal/hepatic venous system compared to the systemic arterial and deep venous systems.
proposals.method: We will conduct a retrospective observational study on a large cohort of acutely ill medical patients hospitalized pre-2026, leveraging detailed electronic health records from the Lombardy region. We will analyze the association between admission serum albumin (a known marker of inflammation and outcome) and other pre-specified biomarkers (e.g., D-dimer, platelet count) with the incidence of site-specific thrombotic events: portal vein thrombosis (PVT), hepatic vein thrombosis (Budd-Chiari), systemic arterial events (MI, stroke), and deep vein thrombosis/pulmonary embolism (DVT/PE). Statistical analysis will employ multivariable Cox proportional hazards models, stratified by vascular bed, adjusting for confounders including age, comorbidities (COPD, cancer), and COVID-19 status.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first large-scale, comparative evidence on the heterogeneity of thrombotic risk across vascular territories in a general medical population. It will inform more targeted surveillance and prophylactic approaches for high-risk patients. The findings will also clarify the pathophysiological link between hypoalbuminemia, systemic inflammation, and site-specific endothelial dysfunction.
proposals.novelty: While prior work by our group and others links low albumin to composite thrombotic outcomes, no study has explicitly tested for differential effects across the portal, hepatic, and systemic circulations within the same patient cohort, challenging the paradigm of uniform hypercoagulability.

proposals.title: Validating the Thrombomodulin-Modified Thrombin Generation Ratio for Thrombosis Risk Reclassification in Cirrhosis: A Prospective Cohort Study
proposals.motivation: Patients with cirrhosis exhibit a complex hemostatic imbalance, with a high risk of both bleeding and thrombosis. Current static laboratory tests and clinical scores like the MELD score inadequately predict thrombotic risk, leading to suboptimal prophylactic strategies. A dynamic functional test, such as the thrombin generation ratio (TGR) with thrombomodulin, could better capture the net pro-thrombotic phenotype, but its clinical utility for reclassifying patient risk remains unproven.
proposals.hypothesis: The addition of the thrombomodulin-modified TGR to a baseline clinical model (including MELD score and prior thrombosis) will significantly improve the reclassification of thrombotic risk in patients with cirrhosis, as measured by the Net Reclassification Improvement (NRI).
proposals.method: We will conduct a prospective, observational cohort study of 500 patients with cirrhosis, with a 2-year follow-up. The primary endpoint is a composite of objectively confirmed portal vein thrombosis, mesenteric vein thrombosis, pulmonary embolism, and thrombotic death. We will construct a baseline Cox proportional hazards model using established clinical variables. The TGR will then be added, and its incremental predictive value will be assessed using metrics of discrimination (C-statistic), calibration, and, primarily, category-free NRI, as established in cardiology biomarker validation studies.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first rigorous, prospective validation of a dynamic hemostatic assay for thrombosis prediction in cirrhosis, moving beyond association to demonstrate concrete clinical utility. It will establish a methodological framework for validating functional biomarkers in hepatology, adapted from cardiology. The results could directly inform future trials of targeted thromboprophylaxis in high-risk cirrhotic patients identified by the TGR.
proposals.novelty: While the TGR's association with thrombosis is known, this study is novel in its direct application of advanced risk reclassification metrics (NRI) from cardiology to demonstrate the assay's clinical value. It also innovates by prospectively validating a pre-defined composite thrombotic endpoint specific to the cirrhotic population.

proposals.title: Systemic Inflammation and Endothelial Activation as Drivers of Elevated Factor VIII and Hypercoagulability in Decompensated Cirrhosis: The Endothelial-Liver Axis
proposals.motivation: Patients with advanced cirrhosis paradoxically exhibit a hypercoagulable state, with elevated plasma factor VIII (FVIII) being a key driver of thrombotic risk, yet its origin remains unexplained as the diseased liver's synthetic capacity is impaired. This mirrors the oncology field, where cytokine-driven endothelial activation explains clotting factor dysregulation, a mechanism unexplored in liver disease. Understanding this is critical, as hypercoagulability contributes to portal vein thrombosis and may worsen outcomes, similar to how hypoalbuminemia predicts poor prognosis in acutely ill medical patients and post-COVID-19 sequelae.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that elevated FVIII in decompensated cirrhosis originates from extra-hepatic vascular endothelial cells activated via an 'endothelial-liver axis,' where portal hypertension and systemic inflammation create a cytokine milieu (e.g., IL-6, VEGF) that stimulates endothelial FVIII synthesis.
proposals.method: We will conduct a prospective observational study of cirrhotic patients hospitalized for acute decompensation, measuring plasma FVIII, cytokines (IL-6, VEGF, TNF-α), and markers of endothelial activation (e.g., von Willebrand factor, soluble ICAM-1). Using multivariate regression, we will model FVIII levels against inflammatory and clinical parameters (e.g., MELD score, portal hypertension signs). In parallel, we will use primary human umbilical vein endothelial cells (HUVECs) in vitro, stimulating them with patient-derived serum or recombinant cytokines and quantifying FVIII mRNA (qRT-PCR) and protein secretion (ELISA), with pathway inhibition (e.g., JAK/STAT, NF-κB) to identify critical signaling.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel pathophysiological mechanism for coagulation dysregulation in cirrhosis, shifting the paradigm from a purely hepatic-centric view to one involving systemic endothelial activation. It will identify specific inflammatory drivers and signaling pathways, providing potential therapeutic targets to modulate thrombotic risk in this vulnerable population, analogous to strategies explored in cancer-associated thrombosis.
proposals.novelty: While inflammation and endothelial dysfunction are recognized in cirrhosis, their direct role in driving FVIII production is unproven. Applying the oncology analogy of cytokine-mediated endothelial synthesis to liver disease represents a novel, non-obvious integration of pre-2026 concepts from distinct fields.

proposals.title: Inflammation, Endothelial Dysfunction, and Portal Hypertension as Drivers of Elevated Factor VIII in Cirrhosis: A Prospective Cohort Study
proposals.motivation: In cirrhosis, elevated plasma factor VIII (FVIII) levels are a recognized prothrombotic risk factor, contributing to the high incidence of portal vein thrombosis. However, the precise pathophysiological mechanisms linking progressive liver disease to increased FVIII synthesis remain poorly understood. Clarifying these drivers is crucial for developing targeted strategies to mitigate thrombotic risk in this vulnerable patient population.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the elevation of FVIII in cirrhosis is driven not by residual hepatic synthesis, but primarily by extra-hepatic endothelial upregulation, which is correlated with the severity of systemic inflammation, endothelial dysfunction, and portal hypertension.
proposals.method: We will conduct a prospective observational study on a cohort of patients with cirrhosis of varying severity (Child-Pugh classes A-C) and matched controls. Plasma FVIII activity will be measured alongside a panel of inflammatory markers (e.g., IL-6, CRP), endothelial dysfunction markers (e.g., von Willebrand factor antigen, soluble thrombomodulin), and direct hemodynamic assessment of portal pressure (hepatic venous pressure gradient, HVPG) where clinically indicated. We will employ multivariable linear regression and path analysis to determine the independent contributions of inflammation, endothelial markers, and portal hypertension to FVIII levels, controlling for liver synthetic function (e.g., albumin, INR).
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a mechanistic map of FVIII elevation in cirrhosis, moving beyond simple correlation to identify primary drivers. It will clarify the relative roles of inflammation versus portal hypertension, offering insights for future anti-inflammatory or anticoagulant trial design. The findings will directly inform risk stratification for thrombosis in cirrhotic patients.
proposals.novelty: While FVIII elevation is known in cirrhosis, this study uniquely integrates direct measures of portal hypertension (HVPG) with a comprehensive panel of inflammatory and endothelial biomarkers in a prospectively characterized cohort to dissect causal pathways, a synthesis not previously performed.

proposals.title: A Dual Proteomic and Functional CRISPR Screen to Identify Causal Hepatic Modifiers of Coagulation in Patients with Cirrhosis
proposals.motivation: Cirrhosis is characterized by a complex, rebalanced hemostasis, leading to unpredictable bleeding and thrombotic risks. Current prognostic markers, like low albumin, are strongly associated with adverse outcomes but are correlative, not causal. Identifying the specific hepatic proteins that mechanistically drive coagulation imbalance is critical for developing targeted therapies and improving risk stratification, akin to the need for mechanistic insights in post-COVID and other acutely ill populations.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that integrating unbiased proteomic discovery in patient samples with functional CRISPR screening in a disease-relevant cellular model will identify novel, causally implicated hepatic secretory proteins that directly modify the coagulation phenotype in cirrhosis.
proposals.method: We will first perform high-throughput proteomic profiling (using Olink or SOMAscan platforms) on plasma and explanted liver tissue from a well-phenotyped cohort of cirrhosis patients stratified by coagulation status. In parallel, we will establish a model of cirrhosis-associated coagulopathy in primary human hepatocytes using cytokine exposure and conduct a pooled CRISPR-Cas9 knockout screen targeting ~2,000 genes encoding secreted proteins, with coagulation factor activity and thrombin generation as functional readouts. Candidates will be prioritized only if they show differential expression in patient proteomics AND a significant functional coagulation perturbation upon knockout in the screen.
proposals.contribution: This work will move the field from association to mechanism by providing a shortlist of high-confidence, causal modifier proteins. It will establish a novel functional screening pipeline for complex coagulopathies and identify potential new therapeutic targets or biomarkers for personalized management of hemostasis in cirrhosis.
proposals.novelty: While proteomic studies of cirrhosis exist, the integration with a phenotype-driven, functional CRISPR screen in a primary human hepatocyte model to establish causality is a novel, non-obvious approach. This directly addresses the limitation of correlative studies highlighted in prior work on albumin and outcomes in acutely ill patients.

proposals.title: Development and Preliminary Clinical Validation of a Paper-Based Lateral Flow Assay for Point-of-Care Thrombin Generation Potential
proposals.motivation: Thrombin generation assays, particularly thrombomodulin-modified tests, are powerful tools for assessing hypercoagulable states, relevant in conditions like COVID-19-associated coagulopathy, COPD exacerbations, and post-hospitalization venous thromboembolism. However, their reliance on complex, laboratory-based fluorogenic instrumentation severely limits clinical utility at the point of care or in resource-limited settings. A simple, rapid, and visual test could transform patient risk stratification and management in acute and post-acute care.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the principles of lateral flow immunoassay (LFIA) technology can be successfully adapted to create a functional, visually read, paper-based cartridge that semi-quantitatively measures thrombin generation potential in capillary whole blood or plasma.
proposals.method: We will design a nitrocellulose-based lateral flow strip with a test zone pre-spotted with recombinant human thrombomodulin. A thrombin-specific, colorigenic peptide substrate (e.g., Tos-Gly-Pro-Arg-p-nitroanilide or a similar chromophore-linked sequence) will be conjugated to gold nanoparticles and dried on a conjugate pad. Upon application of a small (≤50 µL) citrated plasma or directly finger-prick whole blood sample mixed with a buffer containing CaCl2, capillary flow will initiate coagulation and transport generated thrombin. Thrombin cleavage at the test line will release the reporter, producing a visible line whose intensity, measured via a simple smartphone app or visual comparator, correlates with thrombin generation potential.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver the first proof-of-concept for a disposable, instrument-free thrombin generation test, directly translating a complex lab assay to a point-of-care format. It will provide preliminary data on correlation with standard calibrated automated thrombography in cohorts of post-COVID-19 and COPD patients, establishing feasibility for future clinical studies on hypercoagulability risk prediction.
proposals.novelty: While LFIAs are ubiquitous for antigen/antibody detection, their adaptation to measure a dynamic, enzyme-generating proteolytic cascade (thrombin generation) via a cleavable substrate is a novel and non-obvious engineering challenge, distinct from simple capture assays.

proposals.title: Decoupling Hepatic and Endothelial Contributions to DDAVP-Mediated Hemostasis: A Single-Cell Transcriptomic and Proteomic Study in Cirrhosis
proposals.motivation: Desmopressin (DDAVP) is a critical hemostatic agent used in various bleeding disorders, including those in patients with cirrhosis, but its precise cellular mechanism of action remains incompletely defined. Pre-2026 evidence suggests it may stimulate both hepatic synthesis of coagulation factors and endothelial release of von Willebrand factor (VWF), but the relative contribution of each source to its clinical efficacy is unknown. Understanding this is crucial for optimizing therapy and identifying new targets for patients, like those with low albumin or COPD, who may have altered endothelial or hepatic function.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that DDAVP's hemostatic effect in cirrhosis is primarily driven by a coordinated response involving both hepatocyte-derived factor synthesis and liver sinusoidal endothelial cell (LSEC)-specific release of ultralarge VWF multimers, rather than a dominant single-cell-type mechanism.
proposals.method: We will perform single-cell RNA sequencing (scRNA-seq, using 10x Genomics Chromium) and cellular indexing of transcriptomes and epitopes by sequencing (CITE-seq) on liver biopsies from cirrhotic patients before and after DDAVP administration. Hepatocyte and LSEC clusters will be identified using established markers (e.g., ALB, F8 for hepatocytes; CD32b, LYVE1 for LSECs). Differential expression analysis (Seurat, MAST algorithm) will quantify DDAVP-induced changes. These cellular signatures will be correlated with parallel plasma measurements of VWF multimer size (agarose gel electrophoresis) and functional platelet adhesion assays ex vivo.
proposals.contribution: This study will directly decouple the hepatic vs. endothelial contributions to DDAVP's action, providing a high-resolution map of its cellular targets in a clinically relevant population. It will identify specific transcriptional programs and surface proteins associated with a hemostatic response, revealing potential biomarkers for DDAVP efficacy and novel therapeutic targets beyond VWF for managing bleeding risk.
proposals.novelty: While DDAVP's systemic effects are known, applying single-cell multi-omics to human cirrhotic liver tissue pre- and post-DDAVP to dissect its cell-type-specific mechanism is novel. This approach moves beyond plasma-level measurements to directly link cellular source (hepatocyte vs. LSEC) with functional multimer size and platelet activity.

proposals.title: Age- and Hormone-Dependent Thrombotic Risk in Acutely Ill Medical Patients: A Time-Varying Analysis of Serum Albumin and Other Biomarkers
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 evidence, including Dr. Mannucci's work, establishes hypoalbuminemia as a strong, static predictor of thrombotic risk in hospitalized patients. However, thrombotic risk is known to vary with age and hormonal status (e.g., menopause, pregnancy, exogenous hormone use). Current models assume constant risk, potentially misestimating individual patient risk over time and missing critical windows for intervention.
proposals.hypothesis: The thrombotic risk associated with biomarkers like serum albumin is not constant but varies significantly as a function of patient age and hormonal status, with the strength of association peaking during specific high-risk physiological periods.
proposals.method: We will conduct a retrospective cohort study of acutely ill medical patients from Lombardy databases (2019-2025), extending Dr. Mannucci's prior methodology. Using time-to-event analysis, we will employ flexible parametric survival models (Royston-Parmar) or Cox models with time-varying coefficients. Age will be modeled with restricted cubic splines, and hormonal status (sex, menopausal age, pregnancy, hormone therapy) will be included as effect modifiers. The primary outcome is a composite of arterial/venous ischemic events post-admission.
proposals.contribution: This will provide the first quantitative model of how thrombotic risk linked to common biomarkers evolves across the adult lifespan and hormonal transitions. It will offer a more personalized, dynamic risk assessment tool for clinicians, identifying subgroups and specific time periods where monitoring or prophylactic strategies could be most impactful.
proposals.novelty: Moving beyond the static risk paradigm dominant in pre-2026 literature by explicitly modeling how the predictive power of a well-established biomarker (albumin) changes over time due to intrinsic patient factors, not just acute illness.

proposals.title: Time-to-first-thrombosis in acutely ill medical patients: a comparative survival analysis of coagulation defects and hypoalbuminemia
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 evidence shows low serum albumin is a strong predictor of arterial and venous thrombotic events and mortality in acutely ill hospitalized patients. However, the comparative risk and temporal dynamics of thrombosis for patients with specific coagulation defects (e.g., hemophilia carriers, factor deficiencies) versus those with isolated hypoalbuminemia remain poorly quantified. This gap limits personalized thromboprophylaxis strategies in complex medical inpatients.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the time-to-first-thrombosis event will differ significantly between patient groups defined by specific coagulation defects and/or hypoalbuminemia, with hypoalbuminemia conferring a risk comparable to or greater than some inherited defects in the acute medical setting.
proposals.method: We will conduct a retrospective cohort study on acutely ill medical patients hospitalized pre-2026. The primary endpoint is time-to-first radiologically confirmed arterial or venous thrombosis during hospitalization or within 30 days post-discharge. We will apply Kaplan-Meier estimators to generate and compare survival (thrombosis-free) curves for groups defined by coagulation defect type and albumin levels (e.g., <3.0 g/dL). Multivariable Cox proportional hazards models will adjust for confounders including age, COVID-19 status, immobilization, and active cancer, testing the independent association of each defect and albumin level with thrombosis risk.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first head-to-head, time-to-event comparison of thrombosis risk for coagulation defects and hypoalbuminemia in a real-world inpatient cohort. It will quantify the absolute and relative hazards, offering a data-driven hierarchy of thrombotic risk to inform surveillance and prophylaxis protocols. The findings will bridge hematology and internal medicine, highlighting albumin's role as a key biomarker in thrombosis risk stratification.
proposals.novelty: While Dr. Mannucci's prior work established an association between low albumin and thrombosis, applying formal survival analysis to directly compare the temporal risk profiles of albumin and specific coagulation defects in acutely ill patients is novel. This moves beyond cross-sectional risk assessment to a dynamic, clinically actionable model.

proposals.title: A Mendelian Randomization Study to Investigate the Causal Role of Serum Albumin in Arterial and Venous Thrombosis Risk
proposals.motivation: Observational studies, including our own, consistently show that low serum albumin is a strong predictor of arterial and venous thrombotic events and mortality in acutely ill patients, such as those with COPD or post-COVID-19 syndrome. However, it remains unclear whether hypoalbuminemia is a causal risk factor or merely a marker of severe illness and inflammation. Establishing causality is crucial for identifying novel therapeutic targets to prevent thrombosis in high-risk hospitalized and post-hospitalized populations.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that genetically determined lower levels of serum albumin have a causal effect on increasing the risk of both arterial (e.g., myocardial infarction, stroke) and venous (e.g., deep vein thrombosis, pulmonary embolism) thrombotic events.
proposals.method: We will implement a two-sample Mendelian randomization (MR) analysis using pre-2026, large-scale, publicly available genome-wide association study (GWAS) summary statistics. Genetic instruments for serum albumin will be selected from the most recent relevant GWAS (e.g., UK Biobank). Outcome data for thrombotic events will be sourced from consortia like CARDIoGRAMplusC4D (for coronary artery disease) and the INVENT consortium (for venous thromboembolism). We will employ standard inverse-variance weighted MR as the primary analysis, supplemented by sensitivity methods (MR-Egger, weighted median, MR-PRESSO) to assess and correct for potential pleiotropy.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first genetic evidence for or against a causal link between serum albumin and thrombosis, moving beyond observational associations. A positive finding would position albumin not just as a prognostic marker but as a potential modifiable risk factor, informing future interventional studies (e.g., albumin supplementation trials) in high-risk medical patients. It will also demonstrate the application of causal inference methods to our group's core research themes.
proposals.novelty: While MR has been applied to many cardiovascular risk factors, its use to investigate albumin, a key biomarker in acute and chronic illness, in relation to thrombosis is novel. This approach directly tests the causality of an association our group has previously identified observationally in acutely ill and post-COVID patients, integrating genetic epidemiology with our clinical research focus.

proposals.title: A Machine Learning Model Integrating Genetic and Clinical Variables to Predict Thrombosis Risk in Acutely Ill Medical Patients
proposals.motivation: Thrombosis remains a leading cause of morbidity and mortality in hospitalized patients, including those with COPD and post-COVID-19 syndrome. Pre-2026 evidence shows that low serum albumin is a strong predictor of thrombotic events and mortality in this population, but risk stratification remains imprecise. Integrating this established biomarker with genetic predisposition data (e.g., Factor V Leiden, prothrombin mutation) could significantly improve predictive accuracy and enable personalized prophylaxis.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a machine learning model integrating specific genetic defect profiles with routine clinical and laboratory data (including serum albumin) will outperform traditional clinical scores in predicting the risk of arterial and venous thrombosis in acutely ill medical patients during and after hospitalization.
proposals.method: We will conduct a retrospective observational study on a cohort of acutely ill medical patients with available biobanked DNA and clinical data. Features will include genetic variants, admission serum albumin, demographics, comorbidities (e.g., COPD), and hospitalization details. We will train and compare several classification algorithms popular pre-2026, including Random Forest, Gradient Boosting Machines (XGBoost), and regularized logistic regression (LASSO), using nested cross-validation to optimize hyperparameters and prevent overfitting. Model performance will be assessed by AUC, calibration, and net reclassification improvement against clinical benchmarks.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a validated, data-driven tool for thrombosis risk stratification that synthesizes genetic and clinical information. It will offer novel insights into the interaction between genetic predisposition and acquired risk factors like hypoalbuminemia. The resulting model could guide targeted thromboprophylaxis strategies, potentially reducing ischemic events and healthcare resource utilization in high-risk groups.
proposals.novelty: While machine learning has been applied to clinical risk prediction, its integration with specific genetic defect data for thrombosis in a general medical inpatient population is novel. This approach moves beyond single biomarkers or scores to create a multifactorial, personalized risk model, directly building on the PI's expertise in albumin and post-hospitalization outcomes.

proposals.title: A Multinational Registry Study of Thrombophilia Families: Assessing the Long-Term Risk of Arterial and Venous Ischemic Events and the Impact of Acute Illnesses
proposals.motivation: Prior to 2026, evidence from acutely ill medical patients, including those with COVID-19, indicates that low serum albumin is a strong predictor of mortality and ischemic events. However, the long-term thrombotic risk in individuals with inherited thrombophilia, especially during and after acute stressors like hospitalization for infection or COPD exacerbation, remains poorly characterized at a population level. Existing registries are often national and lack the power to study gene-environment interactions and rare outcomes.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that establishing a large, multinational registry of thrombophilia families will reveal that the risk of arterial and venous ischemic events is significantly modulated by acute inflammatory stressors (e.g., viral infections, COPD exacerbations) and that hypoalbuminemia during these episodes acts as a synergistic risk factor.
proposals.method: We will establish a centralized, harmonized data registry by collaborating with clinical centers in Italy, other EU countries, and North America that manage thrombophilia families. Data will include genetic diagnosis, longitudinal clinical history, serum albumin levels, and detailed records of acute illnesses (e.g., COVID-19, COPD exacerbations) and outcomes (ischemic events, mortality). We will employ time-to-event analyses (Cox proportional hazards models) with time-dependent covariates for acute illnesses and albumin levels, and use mixed-effects models to account for familial clustering. Statistical power will be calculated for detecting interactions between thrombophilia status and acute inflammatory triggers.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first large-scale, multinational evidence on the real-world, long-term thrombotic risk profile of thrombophilia families in the context of common acute illnesses. It will clarify the role of hypoalbuminemia as a potential modifiable risk marker in this population. The findings will inform guidelines for monitoring and prophylactic strategies in thrombophilia patients during periods of acute physiological stress.
proposals.novelty: While registries exist, the novelty lies in the specific focus on gene-environment interaction by systematically linking inherited thrombophilia status with acquired, time-limited risk factors (acute illnesses and biomarkers like albumin) captured in a multinational, family-based cohort, building directly on pre-2026 findings in acutely ill general medical patients.

proposals.title: Molecular Mechanisms Linking Hypoalbuminemia to Prothrombotic Risk in Acutely Ill Medical Patients: A Focus on Endothelial and Platelet Dysfunction
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 clinical epidemiology, including Dr. Mannucci's work, robustly links low serum albumin to increased arterial and venous thrombotic risk and mortality in acutely ill patients, such as those with COPD or post-COVID-19 syndrome. However, the causal biochemical pathways remain poorly defined, limiting targeted therapeutic strategies. Moving from association to mechanism is critical to develop interventions beyond albumin replacement.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that hypoalbuminemia, beyond being a marker of inflammation and malnutrition, directly promotes a prothrombotic state by dysregulating endothelial nitric oxide (NO) bioavailability, increasing von Willebrand factor (VWF) release, and enhancing platelet reactivity through altered arachidonic acid metabolism.
proposals.method: We will employ a translational approach using plasma samples from a characterized cohort of acutely ill medical patients (with/without hypoalbuminemia) and in vitro models. Human umbilical vein endothelial cells (HUVECs) and platelets from healthy donors will be exposed to patient-derived plasma or albumin-depleted media. Key assays will include: 1) Endothelial function (NO production via DAF-FM DA fluorescence, VWF antigen and activity), 2) Platelet activation (flow cytometry for P-selectin and activated GPIIb/IIIa, aggregation studies), and 3) Oxidative stress and eicosanoid profiling (mass spectrometry for thromboxane A2 and prostacyclin metabolites). Statistical analysis will use multivariate regression and pathway analysis.
proposals.contribution: This study will delineate specific albumin-sensitive pathways driving thrombosis risk, moving the field from epidemiological observation to mechanistic understanding. It will identify potential molecular targets (e.g., VWF, thromboxane pathway) for pharmacological intervention in high-risk, hypoalbuminemic patients. The findings will provide a biological rationale for future trials testing targeted antithrombotic strategies in this vulnerable population.
proposals.novelty: While albumin's antioxidant and binding properties are known, its specific role in modulating the endothelial-platelet axis in acute illness is unexplored. This proposal uniquely integrates patient-derived samples with targeted cellular assays to dissect causality, bridging Dr. Mannucci's clinical research with molecular hematology.

proposals.title: Association of hypoalbuminemia with microvascular thrombotic events in acutely ill medical patients: a prospective cohort study
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 evidence strongly links low serum albumin to inflammation, endothelial dysfunction, and increased risk of large-vessel venous thromboembolism (VTE) in hospitalized patients. However, the role of albumin in microvascular thrombosis—a critical driver of organ dysfunction in sepsis, severe COVID-19, and COPD exacerbations—remains poorly characterized. This gap is significant as microvascular thrombosis contributes to post-hospitalization morbidity and mortality, yet is not captured by conventional VTE diagnostics.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that in acutely ill medical patients (including those with COPD and post-COVID syndrome), low serum albumin levels are independently associated with an increased incidence of clinically relevant microvascular thrombotic events, measured by a composite endpoint of organ-specific ischemic complications.
proposals.method: We will conduct a prospective observational cohort study of 800 acutely ill medical patients hospitalized in Lombardy, Italy. Serum albumin will be measured at admission. The primary outcome is a composite of microvascular thrombotic events within 90 days post-discharge, defined as acute kidney injury (without macro-embolism), digital ischemia, retinal vein occlusion, or new-onset microvascular angina, adjudicated by a blinded endpoint committee. We will use multivariable Cox proportional hazards regression, adjusting for age, comorbidities, inflammatory markers (C-reactive protein), and conventional VTE risk factors (Padua score). Subgroup analyses will focus on patients with COPD and post-COVID sequelae.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first large-scale epidemiological evidence linking hypoalbuminemia directly to microvascular thrombotic risk, moving beyond the established link to large-vessel DVT. It will offer a novel pathophysiological mechanism for post-acute illness organ damage and identify a simple biomarker (albumin) for risk stratification, potentially guiding future trials of targeted antithrombotic or albumin-replacement strategies in high-risk subgroups.
proposals.novelty: While albumin is known to be associated with large-vessel VTE, its specific role in microvascular thrombosis is unexplored. This proposal innovatively shifts the focus from macrovascular to microvascular beds, integrating the researcher's expertise in albumin, post-COVID outcomes, and hospitalized patient cohorts to address a hidden component of thrombotic risk.

proposals.title: Heterogeneity in Cerebral Venous Thrombosis: A Retrospective Cohort Study of Sinus-Specific Risk Factor Profiles and Clinical Outcomes
proposals.motivation: Cerebral venous thrombosis (CVT) is typically studied as a single entity, but emerging pre-2026 clinical observations suggest potential differences in presentation and etiology based on the thrombosed sinus. Understanding if risk factor profiles differ by anatomical location is crucial for refining diagnostic suspicion and potentially personalizing preventive strategies. This addresses a significant gap, as current guidelines do not stratify management based on sinus involvement.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that CVT is not a homogeneous disease entity and that specific risk factors (e.g., prothrombotic states, infections, hormonal triggers) are differentially associated with thrombosis in distinct cerebral venous sinuses (e.g., superior sagittal, transverse, cavernous).
proposals.method: We will conduct a retrospective observational cohort study of adult patients with radiologically confirmed CVT hospitalized in Lombardy, Italy, from 2015-2023, leveraging regional healthcare databases. Patients will be stratified by the primary thrombosed sinus. Using multivariable logistic regression models, we will assess associations between predefined risk factors (demographics, acute medical conditions including COVID-19 hospitalization, chronic diseases like COPD, cancer, thrombophilia, drug exposures) and specific sinus involvement. Secondary analyses will compare in-hospital mortality, arterial/venous ischemic events, and post-discharge healthcare resource utilization between groups.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first large-scale, systematic analysis of whether CVT risk is sinus-specific, moving beyond the homogeneous disease model. It will inform more targeted etiological work-up and could lay the groundwork for future studies on location-specific prognosis and management. The findings will also extend Dr. Mannucci's expertise in post-hospitalization outcomes and thrombotic risk in acutely ill patients to the neurovascular field.
proposals.novelty: While prior research links low albumin or COVID-19 to general thrombotic risk, no pre-2026 study has rigorously examined if these and other factors have a predilection for specific cerebral venous sinuses, challenging the fundamental assumption of CVT homogeneity.

proposals.title: Risk-Stratified Long-Term Outcomes in Cerebral Venous Thrombosis: A Retrospective Cohort Study Comparing Recurrence and Neurological Sequelae Across Genetic, Hormonal, and Combined Risk Factor Groups
proposals.motivation: Cerebral venous thrombosis (CVT) is a rare but serious condition with significant long-term morbidity. While risk factors like genetic thrombophilias and hormonal exposures (e.g., oral contraceptives, pregnancy) are established, pre-2026 evidence is limited on how these specific etiologies differentially influence long-term prognosis, including recurrence of venous thromboembolism (VTE) and persistent neurological deficits. Understanding these differences is crucial for tailoring post-acute management and follow-up strategies.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that CVT patients with combined genetic and hormonal risk factors will have higher rates of VTE recurrence and more severe long-term neurological sequelae compared to patients with either risk factor alone or those with unprovoked CVT.
proposals.method: We will conduct a retrospective observational cohort study using data from a large regional healthcare database (e.g., Lombardy healthcare utilization data, leveraging the researcher's experience). Adult patients with a first CVT diagnosis (ICD-9/10 codes) from 2015-2023 will be identified and stratified into four groups: genetic (e.g., Factor V Leiden, prothrombin mutation), hormonal, combined, and unprovoked/no identified risk factor. Primary outcomes are VTE recurrence and modified Rankin Scale score >2 at 2-year follow-up. We will employ multivariable Cox proportional hazards and logistic regression models, adjusting for age, sex, acute treatment (anticoagulant drugs), and comorbidities, to compare outcomes between groups.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide novel, risk-stratified long-term outcome data for CVT, directly informing post-discharge monitoring and secondary prevention guidelines. It will clarify the prognostic weight of specific risk factor categories, a gap in pre-2026 literature. The findings will also contribute to a more personalized medicine approach for CVT survivors.
proposals.novelty: While prior studies report general CVT outcomes, a direct, long-term comparison of discrete etiological categories (genetic vs. hormonal vs. combined) is lacking. This study uniquely leverages large-scale real-world data and the researcher's expertise in post-hospitalization outcomes to address this specific stratification.

proposals.title: A Bayesian Adaptive Platform Trial for Genotype-Guided Anticoagulation in High-Risk Medical Inpatients to Prevent Cerebral Venous Thrombosis
proposals.motivation: Cerebral venous thrombosis (CVT) is a devastating complication in acutely ill medical patients, particularly those with inflammatory states like severe COPD or post-COVID-19 syndrome, where low serum albumin is a known marker of hypercoagulability and poor outcomes. Current thromboprophylaxis is applied broadly, with significant bleeding risk, lacking precision for patients with specific genetic thrombophilias (e.g., Factor V Leiden, prothrombin G20210A).
proposals.hypothesis: Targeted, pharmacogenomically-guided anticoagulation in high-risk medical inpatients with both a clinical risk marker (e.g., hypoalbuminemia) and a prothrombotic genetic variant will reduce CVT incidence without increasing major bleeding, compared to standard care.
proposals.method: We propose a multi-center, Bayesian adaptive platform trial. Acutely ill hospitalized medical patients (e.g., with COPD, post-COVID) with serum albumin <3.5 g/dL will be rapidly genotyped. Those positive for pre-specified genetic variants will be randomized within an adaptive framework to genotype-guided prophylactic-dose direct oral anticoagulant (DOAC) or standard pharmacological prophylaxis. Using a response-adaptive randomization algorithm (e.g., Thompson sampling), the trial will adapt allocation probabilities based on interim efficacy (CVT) and safety (major bleeding) endpoints, modeled jointly with Bayesian hierarchical models. Futility and superiority will be assessed via pre-defined posterior probability thresholds.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first prospective evidence for a genotype-stratified prevention strategy for CVT in a high-risk, real-world inpatient population. It will establish a framework for efficient, targeted anticoagulation trials, moving beyond one-size-fits-all prophylaxis. The adaptive design maximizes ethical and operational efficiency, similar to oncology basket trials.
proposals.novelty: This adapts complex, biomarker-driven adaptive trial designs from oncology to the field of venous thromboprophylaxis, specifically for CVT prevention. It uniquely combines a validated clinical biomarker (hypoalbuminemia) with genetic profiling to define a high-risk subgroup for a precision medicine intervention.

proposals.title: Estimating the Population-Specific Penetrance of the Prothrombin G20210A Mutation for Cerebral Venous Thrombosis Using Coalescent Theory and Large-Scale Biobank Data
proposals.motivation: The prothrombin G20210A mutation is a known risk factor for cerebral venous thrombosis (CVT), but its reported risk varies widely across populations. Traditional case-control studies are limited by sample size and ascertainment bias, leading to uncertain and potentially inflated penetrance estimates. A robust, population-genetics-based estimate of its true penetrance is crucial for accurate genetic counseling and understanding the mutation's differential impact on global CVT burden.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the penetrance of the prothrombin G20210A mutation for CVT is significantly lower than commonly reported in the literature and varies predictably across populations based on their demographic history and allele frequency.
proposals.method: We will apply coalescent theory and forward-time population genetics simulations (using SLiM or msprime) to model the expected allele frequency spectrum of the G20210A mutation under different selection coefficients, informed by its known association with CVT. We will use large-scale, pre-2026 biobank data (e.g., UK Biobank, gnomAD) to obtain observed population-specific allele frequencies. By fitting the simulated models to the observed data using approximate Bayesian computation (ABC), we will infer the selection coefficient, which will be directly translated into an estimate of mutation penetrance for CVT.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first population-genetics-derived, ancestry-stratified estimates of prothrombin mutation penetrance for CVT, moving beyond biased clinical samples. It will deliver a novel methodological framework for estimating disease penetrance for other rare variants. The results will directly inform clinical risk assessment and genetic counseling practices across diverse populations.
proposals.novelty: This approach is novel as it leverages population-scale genetic data and evolutionary models to estimate clinical penetrance, circumventing the limitations of traditional clinical epidemiology. It integrates Dr. Mannucci's expertise in thrombosis and post-hospitalization outcomes with a cutting-edge, pre-2026 population genetics methodology.

proposals.title: A Systems Biology Model of Thrombosis Risk Integrating Genetic Mutations and Exogenous Hormones in Post-Hospitalization Patients
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 evidence shows that acutely ill patients, such as those with COPD or post-COVID-19, have high rates of thrombotic events, with low serum albumin being a significant biomarker of risk. Exogenous hormones (e.g., in contraceptives or hormone replacement therapy) are known to modulate coagulation, but their interaction with common prothrombotic mutations (like Factor V Leiden) in a patient-specific context is poorly quantified. A predictive computational model is urgently needed to stratify risk in complex, post-hospitalization populations.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a mechanistic, multi-scale systems biology model can accurately simulate the combined prothrombotic effect of specific genetic mutations and exogenous hormone exposure, and that this integrated risk will correlate with observed clinical outcomes in a cohort of post-hospitalized patients.
proposals.method: We will construct an ordinary differential equation (ODE)-based model of the core coagulation cascade and fibrinolytic system, incorporating key regulatory feedbacks. Model parameters will be calibrated using pre-2026 kinetic data from literature. We will then implement modules to simulate the quantitative effect of specific mutations (e.g., on reaction rates) and hormone exposures (e.g., on synthesis rates of coagulation factors). The model will be validated against retrospective clinical data from post-hospitalized cohorts (leveraging Dr. Mannucci's expertise), using simulated thrombin generation or fibrin formation as outputs correlated with observed ischemic events.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver the first open-source, modular computational framework to simulate personalized thrombosis risk from combined genetic and pharmacological perturbations. It will provide a mechanistic tool to interpret clinical biomarkers like albumin in the context of individual patient profiles. The model will serve as a foundation for testing in silico interventions and guiding personalized thromboprophylaxis strategies.
proposals.novelty: While ODE models of coagulation exist, none are explicitly designed to integrate real-world patient factors like specific mutations and hormone drug exposures into a unified, clinically applicable risk simulation tool, particularly tailored for the complex post-hospitalization population studied by this research group.

proposals.title: Hypoalbuminemia as a Predictor of Site-Specific Venous Thrombosis in Acutely Ill Medical Patients: A Retrospective Cohort Study
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 evidence, including Dr. Mannucci's work, establishes low serum albumin as a strong predictor of composite venous thromboembolism (VTE) and arterial events in hospitalized patients. However, it is unknown if this risk extends uniformly across distinct, clinically significant thrombosis sites like deep-vein thrombosis (DVT) and portal-vein thrombosis (PVT), which have different pathophysiologies and implications for patient management. Clarifying this relationship is crucial for developing more targeted risk assessment and thromboprophylaxis strategies.
proposals.hypothesis: In acutely ill medical patients, hypoalbuminemia is associated with an increased risk of both deep-vein thrombosis and portal-vein thrombosis, but the magnitude of this association differs between these specific venous thrombosis sites.
proposals.method: We will conduct a retrospective observational cohort study using existing electronic health records from a large Italian healthcare network (e.g., Lombardy region), leveraging Dr. Mannucci's experience with such databases. The cohort will include adult medical patients hospitalized pre-2026. We will use multivariable Cox proportional hazards regression, adjusting for established VTE risk factors (age, cancer, immobility), inflammatory markers, and liver function (for PVT analysis), to model the association between admission serum albumin (categorized) and site-specific thrombosis outcomes (DVT and PVT) diagnosed during or within 90 days post-hospitalization.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first detailed evidence on whether albumin's predictive power for VTE is general or site-specific, informing more precise risk stratification models. It will also offer novel epidemiological data on the understudied association between hypoalbuminemia and portal-vein thrombosis in a broad medical population.
proposals.novelty: While hypoalbuminemia is a known marker for general VTE risk, its specific relationship with DVT versus PVT has not been systematically compared, and its role in PVT outside classic cirrhotic populations remains largely unexplored.

proposals.title: The DDAVP-CAST Trial: A Randomized, Controlled Study to Determine if Correction of Bleeding Time Predicts Reduced Clinical Bleeding in Uremic Patients
proposals.motivation: In patients with chronic kidney disease, uremic bleeding is a significant cause of morbidity. Desmopressin (DDAVP) is widely used to correct the prolonged bleeding time, a laboratory surrogate, prior to procedures. However, analogous to the discredited use of arrhythmia suppression for mortality in cardiology (CAST trial), the assumption that this correction reliably prevents clinical bleeding lacks high-level evidence from patient-centered outcome trials.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that in uremic patients undergoing an invasive procedure, treatment with DDAVP to correct bleeding time will not result in a statistically significant reduction in the incidence of clinically significant bleeding events compared to a placebo control.
proposals.method: We propose a multi-center, randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled trial. Acutely hospitalized or post-hospitalization uremic patients (eGFR <30 mL/min) scheduled for a standardized invasive procedure (e.g., kidney biopsy, central line placement) will be randomized to receive a single intravenous dose of DDAVP (0.3 mcg/kg) or matched placebo. The primary endpoint will be the composite incidence of clinically significant bleeding (e.g., major bleeding per ISTH criteria, procedural site complications requiring intervention) within 72 hours. Secondary endpoints will include bleeding time correction, need for transfusion, and healthcare resource utilization. An intention-to-treat analysis will be performed.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first high-grade evidence on the clinical utility of DDAVP for uremic bleeding, directly testing a decades-old practice. It will clarify the predictive value of the bleeding time surrogate, potentially improving patient safety and guiding more rational, evidence-based peri-procedural management in a vulnerable population.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in applying the seminal 'surrogate endpoint fallacy' lesson from cardiology (CAST) to a persistent hematological question, using a rigorous outcomes trial design never before applied to DDAVP's use in uremia.

proposals.title: Phenotype-Specific DDAVP Responsiveness in Uremic Bleeding: A Prospective Cohort Study to Derive a Predictive Hemostatic Profile
proposals.motivation: Uremic bleeding is a common and serious complication in patients with chronic kidney disease, increasing procedural risk and mortality. Current management, including DDAVP, is applied empirically with variable and unpredictable efficacy, as the 'uremic bleeding diathesis' is a heterogeneous syndrome. Pre-2026, there is a critical lack of prospective studies linking specific, measurable hemostatic defects to therapeutic outcomes, hindering personalized care.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that uremic hemostatic dysfunction comprises distinct sub-phenotypes defined by specific patterns of platelet and coagulation factor deficits, and that these sub-phenotypes differentially predict clinical and laboratory response to a standardized DDAVP challenge.
proposals.method: We will prospectively enroll 200 hospitalized patients with CKD stages 4-5 across key etiologies (diabetic nephropathy, glomerulonephritis, polycystic kidney disease). Before and after a 0.3 mcg/kg DDAVP dose, we will perform a comprehensive panel: platelet function (PFA-100, light transmission aggregometry with multiple agonists), viscoelastic testing (TEG/ROTEM), and assays for von Willebrand factor, factor VIII, and uremic toxins (e.g., p-cresol sulfate). Response will be defined by correction of prolonged closure/lysis times and bleeding time. We will use unsupervised clustering (k-means, hierarchical) to identify sub-phenotypes and supervised machine learning (LASSO regression, random forest) to build a predictive model for DDAVP response.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first prospective, multifactorial mapping of hemostatic defects to DDAVP efficacy in uremia. It will deliver a clinically actionable, etiology-aware predictive model to guide targeted hemostatic intervention, moving beyond trial-and-error. The results will establish a framework for phenotype-driven management of coagulopathy in complex, chronically ill populations.
proposals.novelty: This approach moves beyond treating 'uremic bleeding' as a single entity by deconstructing it into mechanistically defined sub-phenotypes. It uniquely integrates a broad pre/post-DDAVP assay panel with etiology-specific enrollment and modern data-driven clustering to create a predictive tool, a significant advance over prior observational studies of isolated hemostatic parameters.

proposals.title: Heterogeneity in DDAVP Hemostatic Response Among Patients with Uremic Bleeding Diathesis: A Prospective Observational Study
proposals.motivation: Uremic bleeding is a common and serious complication of renal failure, and desmopressin (DDAVP) is a standard acute treatment. However, clinical response is variable. Pre-2026 evidence, including Dr. Mannucci's work on albumin and outcomes in acutely ill patients, suggests that patient-specific factors like underlying disease severity and comorbidities significantly influence hemostatic function and treatment efficacy. Understanding the drivers of this variability is crucial for personalized, effective management.
proposals.hypothesis: The hemostatic response to DDAVP in patients with uremic bleeding diathesis is not uniform and varies based on the etiology (e.g., acute kidney injury vs. chronic kidney disease) and severity (e.g., glomerular filtration rate, uremic toxin levels) of renal failure.
proposals.method: We will conduct a prospective observational study of hospitalized patients with renal failure and clinical bleeding signs. Participants will receive a standard intravenous DDAVP dose. Primary outcome: change in bleeding time and PFA-100 closure time pre- and 1-hour post-administration. We will stratify analysis by renal failure etiology (AKI, CKD stages) and severity markers (eGFR, serum creatinine, urea). Using multivariable linear regression, we will model the treatment response, controlling for pre-2026 known confounders like serum albumin, platelet count, and hemoglobin, as per the researcher's prior methodological focus on post-hospitalization outcomes and biomarker analysis.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first systematic evidence that 'uremic bleeding' is not a single entity but a spectrum of hemostatic defects with different therapeutic vulnerabilities. It will move the field towards a stratified treatment approach. The findings will also clarify the role of non-traditional uremic markers in predicting DDAVP efficacy, building on the researcher's expertise in prognostic biomarkers like albumin.
proposals.novelty: While DDAVP use in uremia is established, pre-2026 work largely treats the population as homogeneous. This proposal directly challenges that assumption by testing for response heterogeneity based on clinically accessible renal parameters, a non-obvious refinement given the standard one-size-fits-all therapeutic paradigm.

proposals.title: A point-of-care thrombin generation assay with thrombomodulin to guide personalized DDAVP dosing in uremic patients undergoing surgery: a prospective validation study.
proposals.motivation: Uremic patients have a complex, multifactorial bleeding diathesis, making perioperative hemostatic management challenging. DDAVP is commonly used empirically, but its efficacy is variable and its dosing is not individualized, leading to potential under-treatment or adverse effects like hyponatremia. The lack of a rapid, actionable test to assess the specific hemostatic defect and guide therapy in real-time is a critical unmet need in perioperative care.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that real-time, point-of-care measurement of thrombin generation potential, modified by thrombomodulin to assess the protein C pathway, can identify uremic patients who will benefit from DDAVP and can be used to titrate the dose and timing of administration to achieve target hemostatic levels for specific surgical procedures.
proposals.method: We will conduct a prospective observational study in uremic patients scheduled for surgery. A validated, commercially available point-of-care thrombin generation device (e.g., based on viscoelastic principles with fluorogenic substrate) will be used. Blood samples will be drawn pre-operatively, after initial DDAVP infusion, and intraoperatively as needed. The primary algorithm will correlate baseline endogenous thrombin potential (ETP) with thrombomodulin to a pre-defined DDAVP dosing nomogram. A secondary intraoperative algorithm will trigger a booster dose if ETP falls below a procedure-specific threshold. Outcomes will be compared to a historical cohort managed with standard empirical dosing.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first clinical validation of a POC thrombin generation assay for real-time DDAVP guidance in surgery. It will generate a practical, individualized dosing algorithm to replace empirical practice. The study will directly contribute to the field of precision hemostatic resuscitation, aiming to reduce bleeding complications and blood product use in a high-risk population.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the fusion of a sophisticated, pathway-specific coagulation assay (thrombomodulin-modified TG) with a rapid POC format for real-time perioperative decision-making, moving beyond global tests like bleeding time or standard viscoelastic tests which do not specifically assess the DDAVP-responsive von Willebrand factor/FVIII axis and its regulation.

proposals.title: Combination Therapy with Desmopressin and Conjugated Estrogens for Chronic Uremic Bleeding: A Randomized Controlled Trial on Long-term Efficacy and Tachyphylaxis
proposals.motivation: Uremic bleeding is a major complication of chronic kidney disease, leading to significant morbidity. Desmopressin (DDAVP) is a first-line hemostatic agent, but its utility is limited by tachyphylaxis—a rapidly diminishing response upon repeated dosing—and potential adverse effects like hyponatremia. There is a critical pre-2026 knowledge gap regarding strategies to sustain hemostatic efficacy in patients requiring long-term or recurrent treatment, mirroring the need for optimized protocols seen in other chronic conditions like hemophilia.
proposals.hypothesis: The co-administration of DDAVP with conjugated estrogens will produce a more robust and durable hemostatic effect in chronic uremic bleeding than DDAVP monotherapy, significantly delaying the onset and reducing the severity of tachyphylaxis through complementary mechanisms of action on platelet function and vascular integrity.
proposals.method: We will conduct a prospective, randomized, open-label trial in hospitalized and post-discharge patients with chronic kidney disease (stage 4-5) and a history of recurrent bleeding. Participants will be randomized to receive standard DDAVP monotherapy or DDAVP combined with oral conjugated estrogens for a planned 12-week period. The primary outcome will be the time to development of tachyphylaxis, defined as a ≥50% reduction in the shortening of bleeding time from baseline after a standardized DDAVP challenge dose. Secondary outcomes will include bleeding event rates, transfusion requirements, and safety parameters (e.g., serum sodium, albumin as a marker of inflammation/nutritional status). Serial measurements of bleeding time, von Willebrand factor multimers, and platelet function will be performed.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first high-level evidence on the long-term efficacy of a DDAVP-estrogen combination for uremic bleeding. It will define whether combination therapy can extend the therapeutic window of DDAVP, potentially reducing dosing frequency and improving patient outcomes. The findings will directly inform clinical practice by establishing a data-driven, sustainable treatment protocol for a high-risk population.
proposals.novelty: While DDAVP and estrogens have been studied separately, their synergistic potential for preventing tachyphylaxis in a chronic use setting has not been systematically evaluated in a controlled trial. This approach moves beyond acute management to address the chronic, recurrent nature of the problem, a logical extension of the researcher's prior work on long-term outcomes and biomarkers in acutely ill patients.

proposals.title: Long-term Safety and Tachyphylaxis of Repeated DDAVP Administration in Chronic Uremic Patients: A Retrospective Cohort Study
proposals.motivation: Desmopressin (DDAVP) is used to manage uremic bleeding, a major complication in chronic kidney disease. However, its long-term safety profile and the risk of tachyphylaxis (diminished hemostatic response) with repeated use in this vulnerable population are poorly characterized. This is critical as chronic uremic patients often require multiple interventions and may be at heightened risk for thrombotic events and electrolyte imbalances.
proposals.hypothesis: In chronic uremic patients, repeated administration of DDAVP is associated with a measurable incidence of tachyphylaxis and an increased risk of adverse safety outcomes, particularly hyponatremia and thrombotic events, compared to matched controls.
proposals.method: We will conduct a retrospective observational cohort study using linked administrative healthcare databases from the Lombardy region (Italy). We will identify chronic uremic patients hospitalized between 2015-2023 who received DDAVP. A matched control cohort of uremic patients not receiving DDAVP will be established. Primary outcomes are the incidence of tachyphylaxis (defined as a reduced hemostatic efficacy requiring alternative therapies in subsequent administrations) and safety events (hospitalization for hyponatremia, arterial/venous thrombosis). We will employ Cox proportional hazards models, adjusted for comorbidities (e.g., COPD), serum albumin levels, and concurrent medications, to estimate hazard ratios.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first large-scale, real-world evidence on the long-term risks and efficacy decay of DDAVP in uremia, directly informing clinical guidelines for its repeated use. It will extend the researcher's expertise on albumin and post-hospitalization outcomes to a new therapeutic domain, bridging knowledge from hemophilia and COVID-19 recovery studies to chronic renal disease.
proposals.novelty: While DDAVP tachyphylaxis is known in hemophilia, its systematic evaluation in the distinct pathophysiology of uremia, with a focus on long-term safety using robust, pre-2026 epidemiological methods applied to high-resolution healthcare data, is novel.

proposals.title: Mechanistic Role of DDAVP-Induced von Willebrand Factor Multimer Shift in Correcting Uremic Platelet Dysfunction: A Prospective Observational Study
proposals.motivation: Uremic platelet dysfunction is a major cause of bleeding in patients with chronic kidney disease (CKD). While desmopressin (DDAVP) is used clinically to improve hemostasis by increasing larger von Willebrand factor (VWF) multimers, the precise mechanistic link between this VWF shift and the correction of platelet adhesion and aggregation defects in uremia remains incompletely understood. Elucidating this pathway is critical for optimizing therapeutic strategies in a vulnerable patient population, aligning with Dr. Mannucci's long-standing research on hemostasis and patient outcomes.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that DDAVP corrects uremic platelet dysfunction primarily by increasing the proportion of ultra-large VWF multimers, which enhances platelet adhesion under high shear stress by strengthening the initial GPIbα-VWF bond and facilitating subsequent αIIbβ3 integrin activation, overcoming inhibitory uremic toxins.
proposals.method: We will conduct a prospective observational study in hospitalized CKD patients with documented bleeding diathesis. Blood samples will be taken before and one hour after DDAVP administration. VWF multimer distribution will be analyzed by agarose gel electrophoresis and densitometry. Platelet function will be assessed by PFA-100 closure times, light transmission aggregometry with ristocetin and other agonists, and flow cytometry measuring platelet surface P-selectin and activated αIIbβ3. Shear-dependent platelet adhesion will be quantified using a microfluidic flow chamber coated with collagen. Statistical analysis will employ paired t-tests and linear regression to correlate changes in multimer size with functional platelet metrics.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a detailed, mechanistic map linking specific VWF multimer changes to the functional correction of platelet defects in uremia. It will strengthen the rationale for DDAVP use in CKD and identify potential biomarkers (e.g., specific multimer ratios) for predicting therapeutic response. The findings will contribute to the broader understanding of VWF-platelet interactions in acquired coagulopathies.
proposals.novelty: While DDAVP's effect on VWF is known, this study uniquely integrates detailed multimer analysis with a comprehensive panel of platelet function tests under shear stress specifically in uremic patients, moving beyond correlation to establish a direct mechanistic pathway. This approach is novel compared to prior studies focusing solely on VWF antigen or activity levels.

proposals.title: A High-Throughput Ex Vivo Screening Platform to Identify DDAVP-Responsive Subtypes in Rare Bleeding Disorders
proposals.motivation: DDAVP is a well-established, low-cost hemostatic agent for hemophilia A and type 1 VWD, but its potential in other bleeding diatheses remains largely unexplored. Systematic evaluation is hindered by the rarity and heterogeneity of these disorders. A method to efficiently pre-select likely responders is needed to rationally design clinical trials and expand therapeutic options for patients with limited treatments.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a standardized ex vivo assay panel can identify specific biomarker signatures in rare bleeding disorders (e.g., platelet function defects, certain type 2 VWD variants, mild factor deficiencies) that predict correction of hemostatic parameters by DDAVP.
proposals.method: We will adapt oncology drug-repurposing frameworks to hematology. Blood samples from a well-phenotyped cohort of patients with non-uremic bleeding disorders will be used to create a miniaturized, high-throughput screening panel. Ex vivo whole blood or platelet-rich plasma will be treated with DDAVP and analyzed using standardized assays: light transmission aggregometry (LTA) with multiple agonists, PFA-100/200 closure time, and VWF multimer analysis. Response will be quantified and correlated with clinical and genetic biomarkers using multivariate regression models to define predictive profiles.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a validated ex vivo screening platform to map DDAVP responsiveness across rare bleeding disorders. It will generate a preliminary efficacy dataset and propose biomarker-based criteria to identify patient subgroups for targeted clinical trials, potentially expanding the evidence-based use of this repurposed agent.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in applying a systematic, high-throughput repurposing screening paradigm—common in oncology—to the field of hemostasis, moving beyond anecdotal evidence to a data-driven identification of DDAVP-responsive disorders.

proposals.title: Phenotype-Specific Synergy of DDAVP Combination Therapy for Uremic Bleeding: An Ex Vivo Microfluidic Mechanistic Study
proposals.motivation: Uremic bleeding is a major complication in patients with chronic kidney disease, leading to increased morbidity and healthcare utilization. Current management, often empirical with DDAVP or blood products, lacks precision and is not guided by the specific hemostatic defect, mirroring challenges in other complex coagulopathies like those seen in acutely ill medical patients with hypoalbuminemia. A phenotype-directed approach could optimize therapy and reduce ineffective treatments.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the hemostatic synergy of DDAVP combined with cryoprecipitate or estrogens is not uniform but is maximal when the combination corrects the dominant, patient-specific defect (e.g., platelet dysfunction vs. von Willebrand factor deficiency) identified through advanced laboratory phenotyping.
proposals.method: We will first phenotype patients with severe uremic bleeding using a panel of assays: platelet function analyzer (PFA-100), viscoelastic testing (thromboelastography or ROTEM), and specific factor assays (VWF:Ag, VWF:RCo, fibrinogen). Subsequently, patient blood samples will be perfused over a collagen-coated microfluidic channel at arterial shear rates. Thrombus formation will be quantified in real-time via fluorescence microscopy after treatment with DDAVP alone, cryoprecipitate alone, estrogens alone, and their combinations. Key metrics include platelet adhesion density and fibrin deposition.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first mechanistic, phenotype-specific evidence for rational combination therapy in uremic bleeding, moving beyond a one-size-fits-all approach. It will directly inform the design of a subsequent precision clinical trial targeting the most effective combination to the correct patient subgroup, potentially improving outcomes and resource utilization.
proposals.novelty: While DDAVP and cryoprecipitate are used, their synergistic potential has not been systematically tested in a pathophysiology-directed manner using dynamic ex vivo models. This study integrates detailed clinical phenotyping with microfluidic mechanistic testing to personalize hemostatic therapy, a novel strategy in nephrology.

proposals.title: A Point-of-Care Integrated Hemostasis Assessment Platform: Validation of Combined Real-Time Platelet Adhesion and Thrombin Generation Kinetics in Patients with Hemophilia and Post-COVID-19 Thrombohemorrhagic Dysregulation
proposals.motivation: Current hemostasis testing, like platelet function analyzers and plasma-based thrombin generation, is fragmented, laboratory-bound, and fails to capture the integrated physiology of clot formation. This gap is critical in managing complex hemostatic disturbances, such as those seen in hemophilia (your recent focus) and in patients post-COVID-19 hospitalization, where both thrombotic and bleeding risks coexist, potentially linked to factors like hypoalbuminemia affecting drug distribution and endothelial function.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that an integrated point-of-care device measuring DDAVP-modulated platelet adhesion under flow concurrently with thrombin generation kinetics in a single whole blood sample will provide a superior, physiologically relevant functional hemostasis profile, correlating better with clinical bleeding phenotypes in hemophilia and post-COVID-19 coagulopathy than standard assays.
proposals.method: We will develop a microfluidic chip with two serial modules. First, a miniaturized flow chamber with a collagen/tissue factor-coated surface will use brightfield microscopy and real-time image analysis (via OpenCV-based tracking algorithms) to quantify platelet adhesion. The effluent will enter a second chamber containing thrombomodulin and a fluorogenic substrate (Z-GGR-AMC). Thrombin generation curves (lag time, peak, ETP) will be derived from fluorescence kinetics using a simplified calibration method. The platform will be validated using blood from healthy controls, hemophilia A patients (pre/post DDAVP), and patients recovered from COVID-19 hospitalization.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a first-of-its-kind functional diagnostic that bridges primary and secondary hemostasis at the point-of-care. It will provide a novel tool for personalized therapy monitoring in hemophilia and for risk stratification in complex acquired coagulopathies, directly addressing your research themes on patient outcomes and post-hospitalization sequelae.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the real-time, sequential physical and biochemical integration of two key hemostatic phases in a single, miniaturized device using native whole blood, moving beyond static, plasma-based or isolated cellular tests.

proposals.title: A Controlled Model of Acquired von Willebrand Factor Dysfunction: Reversibility by Desmopressin and Implications for Uremic Bleeding
proposals.motivation: Uremic bleeding is a major clinical complication in patients with chronic kidney disease, linked to acquired von Willebrand factor (VWF) dysfunction. However, studying its reversal by desmopressin (DDAVP) in the clinical uremic environment is confounded by multiple systemic toxins and comorbidities.
